title: Dynamical Fluctuation-Response Relations
abstract: We derive exact dynamical fluctuation-response relations (FRRs) for time-integrated observables of any nonautonomous Markov jump process. The finite-time covariance splits into an initial variability and an integral of response kernels along the driven dynamics. The identity sharpens the dynamical response thermodynamic and kinetic uncertainty relations and fluctuation-response inequalities (FRIs). It also recovers steady-state FRRs, fluctuation-dissipation theorem and Onsager reciprocity, identifies known autonomous FRIs as the zero-frequency mode.
summary: This paper establishes new exact fluctuation-response relations for time-dependent Markov processes. It shows that the covariance of integrated observables can be decomposed into a term representing initial state uncertainty and a term representing the system's response to driving. These new identities allow for tighter bounds on thermodynamic and kinetic uncertainty relations. The framework successfully unifies various known results like the fluctuation-dissipation theorem and Onsager reciprocity within a single dynamical description.
main_problem: The lack of exact fluctuation-response relations (FRRs) for time-integrated observables in nonautonomous (time-dependent) stochastic systems, particularly regarding how initial state variability and driving protocols affect these relations.
main_result: The derivation of an exact dynamical FRR where the finite-time covariance of observables splits into an initial-variability term and an integral of response kernels. This result sharpens existing thermodynamic and kinetic uncertainty relations and recovers steady-state FDT and Onsager reciprocity in the autonomous limit.
method: The authors use finite-time cumulant-generating functions, the backward Kolmogorov equation, and functional derivatives to define response kernels to perturbations in transition rates.
model_or_system: Nonautonomous Markov jump processes on a discrete state space governed by a time-dependent master equation, where transition rates are parameterized by symmetric, antisymmetric, and vertex components.
key_observables: Time-integrated observables including currents, entropy production rate (EPR), and energy-related quantities.
important_parameters: Driving protocols (time-dependent rates), driving speed, driving period, and the initial state distribution variability.
main_assumptions: The system is assumed to be a Markov jump process on a discrete state space; the derivation focuses on linear response to perturbations.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a hierarchy diagram relating the new FRRs to known results like FDT and TURs; Figure 2 presents numerical validation using a two-cycle network, demonstrating probability distribution evolution, variability contributions, and the no-pumping theorem.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of nonautonomous FRRs, defines the Markov jump model and its parameters, derives the exact dynamical FRR and its covariance split, applies the result to sharpen uncertainty relations (TUR/KUR), and provides numerical validation via a two-cycle network.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for open quantum systems and many-body dynamics as it provides exact tools to relate fluctuations to responses in driven, non-equilibrium settings, which is critical for understanding dissipation and precision in driven quantum devices.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Hierarchy of the main results that follow from the dynam- ical FRRs. Red nodes denote exact identities; green nodes denote bounds. Solid outlines indicate results derived in this Letter, while dashed outlines indicate previously known results refined by the initial-variability term, and dotted outlines indicate known results derived without changes. The Dechant–Sasa FRI is marked by ∗be- cause here only its linear-response Markov-jump version is derived.

FIG. 2. (a): Sketch of the two-cycle network, where the red arrows indicate the cycle currents. Starting from 𝒑(0) = (1/4, 1/4, 1/4, 1/4)⊺, the plot shows the evolution of ∥𝒑(𝑡+ 𝑇𝑝) − 𝒑(𝑡)∥under the periodic driving 𝐸𝑛= 𝐸0 cos(2𝜋𝑡/𝑇𝑝−𝜃𝑛) of period 𝑇𝑝= 𝜋/8, as a function of the cycle index 𝑘= 𝑡/𝑇𝑝. (b): The vari- ability contribution ⟨⟨(J𝛾)2⟩⟩0 versus cycle index for 𝛾= 1, 2 (the two curves overlap). (c): In the periodic (Floquet) state, the blue curve validates the no-pumping theorem max𝛾|𝐽𝛾(0,𝑇𝑝)| = 0, the green curve measures the lack of FDT via the norm ∥𝐶−2𝐿∥∞with 𝐶𝛾𝛾′ = ⟨⟨J𝛾, J𝛾′⟩⟩, and the orange curve measures nonreciprocity via |𝐿12 −𝐿21|.
